questions.question: A student's demand for restaurant meals is Q=0.02M/P, where M is monthly income and P is meal price. If M=$1,000 and P=$10, what is the income elasticity of demand, and what does it imply?

choices.A: 1; restaurant meals are normal with unit income elasticity in this demand function.

questions.explanation: Q=0.02M/P is proportional to income, so income elasticity is (dQ/dM)(M/Q)=(0.02/P)(M/(0.02M/P))=1. B, C, and D miscalculate or misclassify the elasticity.

questions.question: Demand for instant noodles is Q=40-0.01M+2P_r, where M is monthly income and P_r is the price of restaurant meals. At M=$2,000 and P_r=$8, what is the income elasticity of demand for instant noodles?
questions.explanation: Q=40-20+16=36. dQ/dM=-0.01. Income elasticity=(-0.01)(2000/36)=-20/36=-0.56. A uses Q=20. C gets the sign wrong. D uses an incorrect quantity or derivative.

questions.question: A good has income elasticity 1.5. If income rises by 8%, approximately what happens to quantity demanded, and what is the mechanism?

choices.A: Quantity rises by 12%; the good is a luxury because demand rises more than proportionally with income.
choices.B: Quantity rises by 5.33%; income is divided by elasticity.
choices.C: Quantity falls by 12%; luxury goods have negative income effects.
choices.D: Quantity rises by 8%; all normal goods rise one-for-one with income.

questions.explanation: Percentage change in Q equals income elasticity times percentage change in income: 1.5×8%=12%. Since elasticity exceeds 1, it is a luxury. B divides instead of multiplying. C gets the sign wrong. D assumes unit elasticity.

questions.question: A bus-pass demand function has income elasticity -0.4. If student income rises by 10%, what happens to bus-pass demand and why?

choices.A: Demand rises by 4% because transportation is necessary.
choices.B: Demand falls by 4% because the good is inferior and higher income shifts demand inward.
choices.C: Demand falls by 40% because elasticity is multiplied by income in dollars.
choices.D: Demand stays unchanged because only price affects demand.

questions.explanation: A 10% income increase changes quantity by -0.4×10%=-4%. Negative income elasticity means inferior good demand falls as income rises. A gets the sign wrong. C misuses percentages. D ignores income as a demand shifter.

questions.question: In an Engel curve graph with income on the vertical axis and quantity of premium coffee on the horizontal axis, quantity increases more than proportionally as income rises. What does this imply about income elasticity?

choices.A: Income elasticity is greater than 1, so premium coffee is a luxury over that range.
choices.B: Income elasticity is between 0 and 1, so premium coffee is a necessity.
choices.C: Income elasticity is negative, so premium coffee is inferior.
choices.D: Income elasticity is zero because price is not on the graph.

questions.explanation: More-than-proportional quantity response to income means income elasticity exceeds 1. B describes less-than-proportional response. C would require quantity falling as income rises. D is false; Engel curves are specifically about income.

questions.question: An Engel curve for discount ramen bends backward: as income rises beyond $1,500 per month, quantity demanded falls. What does this graph feature indicate?

choices.A: Discount ramen becomes inferior over that income range because income elasticity is negative.
choices.B: Discount ramen becomes a luxury because the curve bends.

questions.explanation: A backward-bending Engel curve means higher income reduces quantity demanded, so income elasticity is negative. B misreads curvature as luxury. C concerns price, not income. D does not follow.

questions.question: What does income elasticity of demand equal to 0.3 mean for grocery staples?

choices.A: A 1% increase in income raises quantity demanded by about 0.3%, so the good is normal but income-inelastic.
choices.B: A $1 increase in income raises quantity demanded by 0.3 units.
choices.C: A 1% increase in price lowers quantity demanded by 0.3%.
choices.D: A 1% increase in income lowers quantity demanded by 0.3%, so the good is inferior.

questions.explanation: Income elasticity measures percentage quantity response to percentage income change. A positive value below 1 indicates a normal necessity. B confuses elasticity with derivative. C describes own-price elasticity. D gets the sign wrong.

questions.question: A retailer finds that when local income rises by 6%, demand for its premium headphones rises by 15%. Which model should it use, and how should it classify the good?

choices.A: Income elasticity; elasticity is 2.5, so the headphones are a luxury good.
choices.B: Own-price elasticity; elasticity is -2.5, so demand is elastic.
choices.C: Cross-price elasticity; elasticity is 0.4, so goods are substitutes.

questions.explanation: Income elasticity is 15%/6%=2.5. Since it is positive and greater than 1, the good is a luxury. B uses price elasticity without a price change. C uses the wrong comparative static. D cannot be inferred.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes a normal necessity from a luxury good using income elasticity?

choices.A: A normal necessity has income elasticity between 0 and 1; a luxury has income elasticity greater than 1.
choices.B: A normal necessity has negative income elasticity; a luxury has positive income elasticity below 1.
choices.C: A normal necessity has price elasticity below 1; a luxury has price elasticity above 1.
choices.D: A normal necessity has zero demand response to income; a luxury has zero demand response to price.

questions.explanation: Both are normal goods, but necessities rise less than proportionally with income while luxuries rise more than proportionally. B confuses inferior goods and necessities. C uses own-price elasticity rather than income elasticity. D misstates both categories.

questions.question: This topic does not naturally require Type 7 welfare analysis, so this is a second Type 2. A household's demand for used clothing has income elasticity -1.2. If income falls by 5%, what happens to quantity demanded and what mechanism explains it?

choices.A: Quantity demanded rises by 6% because the good is inferior and lower income increases demand.
choices.B: Quantity demanded falls by 6% because income and demand always move together.
choices.C: Quantity demanded rises by 1.2% because elasticity is the total percentage change.
choices.D: Quantity demanded falls by 5% because income changed by 5%.

questions.explanation: Used clothing has negative income elasticity. A 5% income decrease implies percentage change in quantity = (-1.2)(-5%)=+6%. This replaces Type 7 because income elasticity itself is not a welfare-area concept. B ignores inferior-good behavior. C mistakes elasticity for the quantity change. D assumes unit income elasticity.
